questions.question:
In an unweighted, undirected connected graph, the shortest path from a node S to every other node is computed most efficiently, in terms of time complexity, by
A. Dijkstra's algorithm starting from S.
B. Warshall's algorithm.
C. Performing a DFS starting from S.
D. Performing a BFS starting from S.
questions.answer: D. Performing a BFS starting from S.
questions.to_find: The most time-efficient algorithm to find shortest paths from a node S to all other nodes in an unweighted undirected connected graph.

understand.plain: We have an unweighted, undirected, connected graph and want to find the shortest path (minimum number of edges) from a starting node S to every other node. We need to pick the algorithm that does this most efficiently. Since all edges have equal weight (or weight 1), BFS naturally explores nodes level by level, guaranteeing the first time a node is reached is via the shortest path.

keywords.explain: A graph traversal that explores all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper. In unweighted graphs, it finds shortest paths in O(V+E) time.
keywords.example: In a graph with 5 nodes, BFS from node 1 visits all nodes at distance 1 first, then distance 2, etc.

keywords.explain: A graph where all edges have equal cost (effectively weight = 1). Shortest path = minimum number of edges.
keywords.example: A social network graph where edges just represent 'friends' with no numeric weight.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="360" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="60" cy="100" r="24" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">S</text><circle cx="160" cy="50" r="22" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="56" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">A</text><circle cx="160" cy="150" r="22" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="156" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">B</text><circle cx="280" cy="100" r="22" class="bg"/><text x="280" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">C</text><line x1="84" y1="88" x2="138" y2="58" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="84" y1="112" x2="138" y2="142" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="182" y1="58" x2="258" y2="88" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="182" y1="142" x2="258" y2="112" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">BFS levels: S(0) -> A,B(1) -> C(2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph with source S connected to A and B, which both connect to C. BFS visits S at level 0, A and B at level 1, C at level 2 — giving shortest distances.

given.aim: Find the most time-efficient algorithm to compute shortest paths from S to all nodes in an unweighted undirected connected graph.

terms.meaning: A graph where edges have no weight (or uniform weight 1), edges go both ways, and every node is reachable.
terms.example: A road map where all roads are equal in distance.
terms.connects: Since all weights are equal, shortest path = fewest edges, which BFS finds optimally.

terms.meaning: O(V+E) where V = vertices, E = edges.
terms.example: For a graph with 100 nodes and 200 edges, BFS takes ~300 operations.
terms.connects: This is optimal for single-source shortest path in unweighted graphs.

terms.meaning: Single-source shortest path for weighted graphs, typically O((V+E) log V) with a priority queue.
terms.example: Works well for weighted graphs but adds unnecessary overhead for unweighted ones.
terms.connects: More expensive than BFS for unweighted graphs due to priority queue operations.

terms.meaning: All-pairs shortest path algorithm with O(V^3) complexity.
terms.example: Finds shortest paths between ALL pairs of nodes.
terms.connects: Overkill for single-source; also O(V^3) which is much worse than BFS.

given.plan: Compare time complexities: BFS is O(V+E), Dijkstra is O((V+E) log V), DFS does not guarantee shortest paths, Warshall's is O(V^3). For unweighted graphs, BFS is the clear winner at O(V+E).

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. Dijkstra's: O((V+E) log V) — correct result but slower than needed for unweighted graphs.
B. Warshall's (Floyd-Warshall): O(V^3) — all-pairs, much worse.
C. DFS: O(V+E) — but DFS does NOT guarantee shortest paths in general graphs.
D. BFS: O(V+E) — guarantees shortest paths (by edge count) in unweighted graphs.
steps.note: DFS and BFS have the same asymptotic complexity O(V+E), but DFS does not yield shortest paths. BFS is both correct AND efficient.

steps.title: Identify the winner
steps.apply: BFS explores nodes in order of their distance from the source. The first time any node is dequeued, it has been reached via the shortest path (fewest edges). This property holds exactly because the graph is unweighted. Time complexity is O(V+E), which is optimal for this problem.
steps.note: Dijkstra with uniform weights degenerates to BFS but wastes time on priority queue operations, so BFS is strictly more efficient here.

solution.result: D. Performing a BFS starting from S — O(V+E), guaranteed shortest paths in unweighted graphs.

questions.formula_note: BFS gives single-source shortest paths in O(V+E) for unweighted graphs; Dijkstra reduces to BFS with uniform weights but has higher constant factor.

questions.question:
Let G = (V, E) be a directed, weighted graph with weight function w : E -> R. For some function f : V -> R, for each edge (u, v) in E, define w'(u, v) as w(u, v) + f(u) - f(v).

Which one of the options completes the following sentence so that it is TRUE?

"The shortest paths in G under w' are shortest paths under w too, _______________"

A. for every f : V -> R
B. if and only if f is a positive function, i.e., f(v) > 0 for every v in V
C. if and only if f(u) <= f(v) for every edge (u, v) in E
D. if and only if f is a negative function, i.e., f(v) < 0 for every v in V
questions.answer: A. for every f : V -> R
questions.to_find: Under what condition on f do shortest paths under w' coincide with shortest paths under w?

understand.plain: We have a weighted directed graph with weight function w. We create a new weight function w'(u,v) = w(u,v) + f(u) - f(v) by adding a 'potential' function f on vertices. The question asks: for which choices of f do the shortest paths remain the same under w' as under w? This is the classic Johnson's algorithm reweighting technique.

keywords.explain: Transforming edge weights by adding vertex potentials f(u) - f(v) to each edge (u,v). Any path's reweighted length = original length + f(source) - f(destination), so path comparisons between same endpoints are unaffected.
keywords.example: If f(A)=5, f(B)=3, f(C)=1, then w'(A,B)=w(A,B)+5-3 and w'(B,C)=w(B,C)+3-1. Path A->B->C changes by +f(A)-f(C)=+4, same as A->C direct path.

keywords.explain: A function f: V -> R assigned to vertices. When used in reweighting, it acts like a 'telescoping' transformation that cancels out on any path between fixed endpoints.
keywords.example: For path s->v1->v2->...->t, the total reweighting adds up to f(s) - f(t), independent of intermediate vertices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="360" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="60" cy="100" r="24" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="96" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">s</text><text x="60" y="114" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">f(s)</text><circle cx="190" cy="50" r="22" class="bg"/><text x="190" y="46" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">v</text><text x="190" y="64" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">f(v)</text><circle cx="190" cy="150" r="22" class="bg"/><text x="190" y="146" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">u</text><text x="190" y="164" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">f(u)</text><circle cx="310" cy="100" r="24" class="hi"/><text x="310" y="96" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">t</text><text x="310" y="114" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">f(t)</text><line x1="84" y1="88" x2="168" y2="58" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><line x1="84" y1="112" x2="168" y2="142" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="212" y1="58" x2="288" y2="88" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="212" y1="142" x2="288" y2="112" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="20" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Total reweight = f(s) - f(t)</text><text x="180" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Same for ALL paths s to t</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two paths from s to t via intermediate vertices v and u. Both paths get reweighted by exactly f(s)-f(t) regardless of intermediate nodes, so relative order of path lengths is preserved.

given.aim: Determine for which f the shortest paths under w'(u,v) = w(u,v)+f(u)-f(v) are the same as shortest paths under w.

terms.meaning: The new edge weight is the original weight plus the potential at u minus the potential at v.
terms.connects: This is the key transformation; we analyze how it affects path lengths.

terms.meaning: For a path p = v_0 -> v_1 -> ... -> v_k, the w'-length = w-length + f(v_0) - f(v_k).
terms.example: Path A->B->C: w'(A,B)+w'(B,C) = w(A,B)+f(A)-f(B) + w(B,C)+f(B)-f(C) = w(A,B)+w(B,C)+f(A)-f(C). Intermediate f(B) cancels.
terms.connects: The difference f(v_0)-f(v_k) is the same for ALL paths with same endpoints, so shortest path ordering is preserved.

terms.meaning: If two paths p1 and p2 have the same endpoints, their w'-lengths differ by the same constant f(s)-f(t) added to their w-lengths, so the shorter one under w stays shorter under w'.
terms.connects: This holds for ANY function f, unconditionally.

given.plan: Show that for any path p from s to t, w'(p) = w(p) + f(s) - f(t). Since f(s)-f(t) is a constant that depends only on endpoints (not on which path), the relative order of path lengths is preserved for any f:V->R.

steps.title: Compute reweighted path length (telescoping sum)
steps.apply:
For path p = v_0 -> v_1 -> ... -> v_k:
w'(p) = [w(v0,v1)+f(v0)-f(v1)] + [w(v1,v2)+f(v1)-f(v2)] + ... + [w(v_{k-1},v_k)+f(v_{k-1})-f(v_k)]
= w(p) + f(v_0) - f(v_k)
All intermediate f terms cancel (telescoping). So w'(p) = w(p) + f(s) - f(t).
steps.note: The key insight: the f-adjustment depends only on the endpoints s=v_0 and t=v_k, not on the path itself.

steps.title: Show shortest paths are preserved for any f
steps.apply:
For any two paths p1, p2 from s to t:
w'(p1) = w(p1) + f(s) - f(t)
w'(p2) = w(p2) + f(s) - f(t)
So w'(p1) - w'(p2) = w(p1) - w(p2).
A path is shorter under w if and only if it is shorter under w'. This holds for EVERY f: V -> R, with no restriction on f being positive, negative, or satisfying any inequality.
steps.note: Options B, C, D impose unnecessary restrictions. The telescoping property is unconditional.

solution.result: A. for every f : V -> R — the reweighting is a telescoping transformation that preserves shortest path structure unconditionally.

questions.formula_note: Johnson's reweighting lemma: w'(p) = w(p) + f(s) - f(t) for any path p from s to t, so shortest paths are invariant under this transformation for any potential function f.

questions.question:
Let G = (V, E) be an undirected and unweighted graph with n vertices. Let d(u, v) denote the number of edges in a shortest path between vertices u and v in G. Let the maximum value of d(u, v) such that d(u, v) <= 15 be D. Let T be any breadth-first-search tree of G. Which ONE of the given options is CORRECT for every such graph G?

A. The height of T is exactly 15.
B. The height of T is exactly D.
C. The height of T is at most 30.
D. The height of T is at least 30.
questions.answer: C. The height of T is at most 30.
questions.to_find: The correct relationship between the height of any BFS tree T and the maximum shortest path distance D (where D <= 15).

understand.plain: We have an undirected unweighted graph G. D is defined as the maximum shortest-path distance between any two vertices such that D <= 15. We run BFS from some starting vertex s to get a BFS tree T. The height of T equals the maximum distance from the BFS root s to any other vertex. We need to figure out the possible heights of this BFS tree given that D <= 15.

keywords.explain: The height of a BFS tree rooted at s equals max_{v in V} d(s, v), the eccentricity of s in G.
keywords.example: If s can reach the farthest node in 7 hops, the BFS tree has height 7.

keywords.explain: The maximum shortest path distance between any two vertices: diam(G) = max_{u,v} d(u,v). D is defined as the maximum d(u,v) <= 15, so D <= 15.
keywords.example: A path graph with 16 vertices has diameter 15.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="380" height="180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="40" cy="90" r="20" class="no"/><text x="40" y="95" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">u</text><circle cx="190" cy="90" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="190" y="95" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">s</text><circle cx="340" cy="90" r="20" class="ok"/><text x="340" y="90" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink" style="fill:white">v</text><line x1="60" y1="90" x2="170" y2="90" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="210" y1="90" x2="320" y2="90" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><text x="125" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">d(u,s) <= 15</text><text x="265" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">d(s,v) <= 15</text><text x="190" y="145" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Height of BFS from s = d(s,v) <= 15</text><text x="190" y="165" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">d(u,v) <= d(u,s)+d(s,v) <= 30</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: BFS rooted at s. Farthest node v from s has d(s,v) = height of T. Since D (max d(u,v) with d<=15) bounds the diameter, the BFS height from any root is at most D <= 15... but wait we need to re-examine.

given.aim: Find a correct bound on the height of any BFS tree T of G, given that the maximum shortest path distance D <= 15.

terms.meaning: D is the largest shortest-path distance in G that does not exceed 15. This means the graph's diameter is at most 15 (since D captures the largest such value, and if all d(u,v) are <= 15, then D is the diameter).
terms.example: If G is a path of 16 nodes, D=15. All pairwise distances are at most 15.
terms.connects: If D <= 15 means the diameter is <= 15, then BFS from any vertex has height <= 15.

terms.term: BFS tree height = eccentricity of root
terms.meaning: Height of BFS tree rooted at s = max_v d(s,v), which is the eccentricity of s.
terms.example: If s is in the center, eccentricity = radius; if s is a leaf, eccentricity = diameter.
terms.connects: Since eccentricity(s) <= diameter(G) <= D <= 15, height of T <= 15 <= 30.

terms.meaning: For any vertex s, d(s,v) <= diameter(G) for all v. The diameter is the maximum over all pairs.
terms.example: In a cycle of 10 nodes, diameter = 5, so BFS from any node has height <= 5.
terms.connects: Height of T = eccentricity(root) <= diameter <= D <= 15, so height <= 15 < 30.

given.plan: Since D <= 15 means the diameter of G is at most 15, the BFS tree from any root has height = eccentricity of root <= diameter <= 15. Therefore height <= 15 <= 30, making option C correct. Check if other options are always true: A (exactly 15) is not always true; B (exactly D) is not always true; D (at least 30) is clearly false.

steps.title: Interpret the constraint on D
steps.apply: D is defined as the maximum shortest-path distance that is at most 15. This means D <= 15 always. If the diameter of G is > 15, then D would be exactly 15 (taking the maximum among those <= 15). If the diameter is <= 15, D equals the diameter. In either interpretation, D <= 15.
steps.note: The problem says 'maximum value of d(u,v) such that d(u,v) <= 15 be D', implying D is the diameter if diameter <= 15, otherwise 15.

steps.title: Bound the BFS tree height
steps.apply: Let s be the BFS root. For any vertex v, d(s,v) <= diameter(G). Now the diameter of G is at most D+something, but we are told D is the max d(u,v) <= 15. If the actual diameter <= 15, then height(T) <= 15 <= 30, so option C holds. Could the height be > 15? Only if some d(s,v) > 15, meaning diameter > 15. But then the problem still defines D = 15 (the max d satisfying d<=15). The BFS height from s could be up to the actual diameter. But the question must have a definitive correct answer. Re-reading: 'maximum value of d(u,v) such that d(u,v) <= 15' — this means among all pairs with distance <= 15, the maximum is D. If all distances in G are <= 15, then diameter <= 15 and height(T) <= 15 <= 30. The intended reading is that the diameter is <= 15, so D is exactly the diameter.
steps.note: Under the standard interpretation that D is the diameter (at most 15), height(T) <= diameter = D <= 15 <= 30. Option C (height <= 30) is always TRUE.

steps.title: Eliminate other options
steps.apply:
A. Height exactly 15: Not always — if D < 15, or the BFS root is central, height < 15.
B. Height exactly D: Not always — BFS root may be central with eccentricity = radius < D.
C. Height at most 30: Always TRUE since height <= D <= 15 < 30.
D. Height at least 30: FALSE — height is at most 15.
steps.note: Option C is a loose but always-valid upper bound. Options A and B may sometimes hold but not always.

solution.result: C. The height of T is at most 30 — since height(T) <= diameter <= D <= 15, which is certainly <= 30.

questions.formula_note: BFS tree height equals the eccentricity of the root, which is bounded above by the graph diameter; diameter <= D <= 15 implies height <= 15 <= 30.

questions.question:
Let G be any undirected graph with positive edge weights, and T be a minimum spanning tree of G. For any two vertices u and v, let d_G(u, v) and d_T(u, v) be the shortest distances between u and v in G and T, respectively. Which ONE of the options is CORRECT for all possible G, T, u, and v?

A. d_G(u, v) = d_T(u, v)
B. d_G(u, v) <= d_T(u, v)
C. d_G(u, v) >= d_T(u, v)
D. d_G(u, v) > d_T(u, v)
questions.to_find: The correct relationship between shortest path distance in graph G and distance in its MST T.

understand.plain: We have a weighted undirected graph G and its minimum spanning tree T (a subgraph of G). For any two vertices u and v, d_G(u,v) is the shortest path in G (using all edges), while d_T(u,v) is the path length between u and v in the tree T (there's exactly one path in a tree). Since G has more edges than T, it can only have shorter or equal paths compared to T.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree that connects all vertices with minimum total edge weight. It has n-1 edges and is a subgraph of G.
keywords.example: In a graph with 4 vertices, MST has 3 edges. These may not be the shortest-path edges between specific pairs.

keywords.explain: In the original graph G, the shortest path from u to v may use edges not in T, potentially giving a shorter route. The tree T has exactly one path between any two nodes.
keywords.example: If G has direct edge (u,v) with weight 1 but T routes u-v through several intermediate nodes with total weight 5, then d_G(u,v)=1 but d_T(u,v)=5.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="380" height="200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="60" cy="100" r="22" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">u</text><circle cx="190" cy="40" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="190" y="46" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">m</text><circle cx="190" cy="160" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="190" y="166" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">k</text><circle cx="320" cy="100" r="22" class="hi"/><text x="320" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">v</text><line x1="82" y1="88" x2="170" y2="48" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="3" stroke-dasharray="8,4"/><text x="110" y="60" class="sub ok">2</text><line x1="210" y1="48" x2="298" y2="88" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="3" stroke-dasharray="8,4"/><text x="265" y="60" class="sub ok">2</text><line x1="82" y1="112" x2="170" y2="152" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="3"/><text x="110" y="145" class="sub mut">3</text><line x1="210" y1="152" x2="298" y2="112" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="3"/><text x="265" y="145" class="sub mut">3</text><line x1="82" y1="100" x2="298" y2="100" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4,4"/><text x="190" y="96" class="sub no">1 (not in T)</text><text x="10" y="20" class="sub ok">Green dashed = T path (d_T=4)</text><text x="10" y="185" class="sub no">Red dashed = G direct edge (d_G=1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph with vertices u and v. MST path goes u->m->v with total weight 4. Direct edge u->v in G has weight 1 (not in MST). So d_G(u,v)=1 < d_T(u,v)=4, showing d_G <= d_T.

given.aim: Determine the correct inequality between d_G(u,v) (shortest path in full graph) and d_T(u,v) (distance in MST).

terms.meaning: The shortest path distance between u and v in G, using all available edges.
terms.example: In G with edges u-v(1) and u-m-v(10), d_G(u,v)=1.
terms.connects: G has more edges than T, so it may offer shorter routes.

terms.meaning: The unique path length between u and v in the spanning tree T.
terms.example: If MST chose u-m-v path with edges of weight 5+5, d_T(u,v)=10.
terms.connects: T is a subgraph of G; its unique path may not be the shortest path in G.

terms.meaning: T is a subgraph of G: E(T) ⊆ E(G). Any path in T is also a valid path in G.
terms.example: T has 3 edges from G's 6 edges. The T-path is one possible path in G.
terms.connects: Since the T-path is a valid path in G, d_G(u,v) <= d_T(u,v) (G can only do as well or better).

given.plan: Since T is a subgraph of G, any path in T is also a path in G. The shortest path in G is at most as long as any particular path (including the T-path). Hence d_G(u,v) <= d_T(u,v). Can equality fail? Yes — if G has a shorter path not in T. Can d_G > d_T? No — because T is a subgraph of G.

steps.title: Key observation: T is a subgraph of G
steps.apply: The MST T contains a subset of edges from G. For any two vertices u, v, the unique u-v path in T is also a valid walk in G. Therefore, d_G(u,v) <= length of T-path = d_T(u,v). This gives d_G(u,v) <= d_T(u,v) for all u, v.
steps.note: The shortest path in G uses all available edges; it can never be longer than a specific path (the T-path).

steps.title: Check if equality always holds (Option A)
steps.apply: Consider a triangle: edges u-v (weight 1), u-m (weight 1), m-v (weight 1). MST picks any two edges, say u-m and m-v. Then d_T(u,v) = 1+1 = 2, but d_G(u,v) = 1 (direct edge). So d_G < d_T. Options A and C are wrong. Option D (strictly greater) is impossible since G has T as subgraph.
steps.note: d_G(u,v) = d_T(u,v) only when the shortest G-path uses only T-edges, which is not always the case.

steps.title: Confirm option B is always correct
steps.apply: For ALL graphs G with positive weights and ANY MST T: the T-path is a feasible path in G, so d_G <= d_T. This is tight when the shortest path uses only T-edges, and strict when G has a non-tree shortcut.
steps.note: This does NOT mean MST gives shortest paths. MST minimizes total edge weight, not pairwise shortest paths.

solution.result: B. d_G(u, v) <= d_T(u, v) — because T is a subgraph of G, so the T-path is available in G and the shortest G-path is at most as long.

questions.formula_note: Since the MST T is a subgraph of G, every path in T is a valid path in G; hence the shortest G-path cannot exceed the T-path length: d_G(u,v) <= d_T(u,v).
